title: Adolf von Harnack and “What is Christianity?”
author: https://hedgehogreview.com/contributors/jonathan-d-teubner
text:
The waning years of the nineteenth century found Germany on edge, balanced between looming crises and emerging opportunities. In 1890, Kaiser Wilhelm II effectively forced Chancellor Otto von Bismarck, the architect of German unity and the father of modern Germany, to resign. Throughout the ensuing decade, German politics were roiled by increasingly violent clashes between conservative aristocratic elements and a powerful social democratic movement, which in 1891 formally adopted a Marxist program of revolution. During the same years, the German economy was emerging as the most dynamic in Europe. Reversing the trend of emigration to the Americas, young men were now flocking to new factories opening in Berlin and the Ruhr region. By 1893, the nation’s steel production exceeded that of Britain, which only two decades before had been doubling Germany’s output. The combined pressures of political dysfunction, relative economic strength, and underlying class tensions produced a culture and society at a precarious crossroads. And Germans knew it. 

During those hinge decades, Germany’s well-stocked bench of sociologists, philosophers, and theologians attempted to address the various sources of the nation’s febrile condition—the new discipline of sociology having been very much a product of this era, with Émile Durkheim, Georg Simmel, and Max Weber each contributing critical founding texts to their fledgling field. Questions animating this discipline—including ones about social cohesion and values—drove similar debates in the older disciplines of philosophy and theology. An influential branch of neo-Kantians—the “Southwestern School”—proposed that the central topic of philosophy should be values, confidently arguing for universally held norms on which an all-encompassing philosophy of culture could be built. In theology, the now little-known systematic theologian Albrecht Ritschl and his students advocated for a spiritual and ethical religion based on the life and experience of Jesus. In each of these efforts, we find attempts to locate an underlying moral or spiritual account that could bring order to a German society rushing into modernity at ever-increasing—and often reckless—speed. 

So it should be no surprise that, in the winter semester of 1899–1900, more than six hundred students, faculty, and associated members of the University of Berlin filed into a lecture hall to hear Adolf Harnack, later to be ennobled as von Harnack, speak about the “essence” of Christianity. Harnack held the chair in Ancient Christianity at the University of Berlin; in 1905, he would be named the director-general of the Prussian Royal Library and later would serve as the founding president of the Kaiser Wilhelm Gesellschaft (renamed the Max Planck Society). He was well-known internationally for his lectures on the essence of Christianity as well as for his textbook, The History of Dogma, and his more than sixteen hundred scholarly and popular works. Harnack not only epitomized the supreme achievement of German culture but was also increasingly seen as the leading figure in liberal Protestantism, a movement that was, at the time, the most engaging intellectual response to the crisis of meaning in German society.

In sixteen lectures—published later in the year as Das Wesen des Christentums, “the essence of Christianity,” and soon translated into English as What Is Christianity?—Harnack sought to provide a “purely historical” answer. Eschewing both “apologetic” accounts and anything that smacked of a “philosophy of religion,” he proposed that the task of the Christian historian was to discern the essential in the totality of Jesus’s life and teaching. This would require what he called a “comprehensive induction”—a full survey of not only the life and teaching of Jesus but also the effect it had, first, on his disciples and, subsequently, on the Church through history. By doing so, Harnack sought to return Germany to the simple message of Jesus. 

Harnack held that the essence of the Gospel could be found in three bold claims: “the kingdom of God and its coming,” “God the Father and the infinite value of the human soul,” and “the higher righteousness and the commandment of love.” In these three claims, complex and contestable as they are, Harnack found the “essence” or “kernel” underlying Jesus’s life and teaching. Everything else must be stripped away as mere “husk.” Such chaff included not just the technical vocabulary theologians use to speak about the being and activity of God, including the identity of Christ himself, but also the rituals and political forms Christian churches take on. One must peer behind these outward political forms to find the heart of the religion. 

The essence of Jesus’s message was, for Harnack, radically individual, found exclusively in the private, intimate relation between the soul and God. The singular force of Harnack’s faith—and the golden thread running through his lectures on the essence of Christianity—is the propulsion of the individual soul toward God. Ignore all that is going on around you, he as much counseled; arcane theoretical efforts to understand the world will get you only so far. The simple message of Jesus is all that you need to hear to encounter afresh the life of Jesus—a life that was singularly dedicated to communion with God the Father. Heeding that message would revivify one’s soul and thereby transform culture through individual acts of “brotherliness” and love. 

Harnack’s “simple” message earned him a wide readership, and initiated a flurry of emulators. The German version of his book sold more than one hundred thousand copies in its first three editions. Although reliable figures are hard to come by, evidence suggests his readership in the United States was even larger. When Harnack, Max Weber, and Ernst Troeltsch journeyed to America for the World’s Fair in 1904, Harnack drew the largest crowds. Weber’s own landmark text, The Protestant Ethic and the Spirit of Capitalism, would not appear in English until 1930. 

Today, you could imagine Harnack’s What Is Christianity? being sold in airports or even adapted as a form of pop spirituality. Yet even during his own lifetime, deeply unsettling events would cause a rethinking of Harnack’s “simple” Gospel. Like others of his generation, the popular scholar is now read with the catastrophe of World War I in view, not least because he was one of the signers of the Manifesto of the Intellectuals proclaiming support for Germany at the start of the Great War. Originally titled “To the Cultured World!” (An die Kulturwelt!), the manifesto bore the signatures of the leading German intellectuals of the day, a rarefied circle at the center of which was Harnack himself. 

To be sure, to be a man of culture in Wilhelmine Germany was also to be a political animal. Harnack was an adviser to the Kaiser and weighed in on academic appointments. But perhaps more consequentially, Harnack drafted substantial portions of Kaiser Wilhelm II’s speech before an extraordinary assembly of the Reichstag. The Kaiser talked about the moral case for the war—for Germany’s right to defend itself and, by extension, “to defend the place in which God has put Germany,” words that could only have been written by the Kaiser’s favorite theologian. Such political involvements disappointed not only Harnack’s foreign colleagues and correspondents, many of whom had looked to him as one of the leading theologians of his day, but also a whole generation of students, most significantly the Swiss theologian Karl Barth. For Barth and others, Harnack stood for the politicalization of religion. 

But none of this was quite predictable in 1900, when he stood at the lectern in the great hall at the University of Berlin and proclaimed an individualized and private religion. Indeed, his simple Gospel message would seem to have called his future political movements into question. He was clear throughout his lectures that he did not see Jesus laying down any social program. Nor did he suggest that it was right to see Jesus as a political revolutionary. Even while acknowledging that the Gospel was profoundly socialistic, he insisted that it was so only because it was profoundly individualistic. The infinite and independent value of every human soul demanded a universal community above and beyond the conflicting interests of politics. Any effort to drag the Gospel into the messiness of politics would spoil its purity. 

Indeed, it is possible to read the entire “essence” lectures as a diatribe against “political religion.” The entanglement of the early Jesus movement with political regimes can be found, Harnack argued, from the very beginning, in what he described as the movement’s “Jewish epoch.” It becomes clear at one point in the lectures that the paradigm case of political religion was not Constantinianism or even Caesaropapism but Judaism. “The Husk” he wrote, “was the whole of the Jewish context of Jesus’s message; husks were also such definite statements as ‘I am not sent but unto the lost sheep of the house of Israel.’” Harnack did not deny the Jewishness of Jesus or even that his followers were considered a sect of Judaism, both of which would be denied by Nazi theologians in only a few decades. He did, however, argue that close examination of the Gospels reveals a teaching that is often at odds with Judaism, that the Jewish context obscured the Gospels. For Harnack, the troubling aspect of “political religion” is not that the faith was bound up with political forms but that it sanctified a particular political formation and bound the Gospels to that context and circumstance. Harnack’s criticism of Judaism can also be seen as informing his critiques of Greek Orthodoxy and Roman Catholicism—that they are weighed down by traditionalism and ritualism and had, in different ways, sanctified a political form as a special bearer of those traditions and rituals. In Harnack’s thinking, “political religion” was responsible for trapping Jesus’s message in a particular epoch. 

Today, with the advantage of hindsight, we might find the most troubling aspect of Harnack’s lectures in his account of Christianity’s separation from Judaism, or what is often referred to as the “parting of the ways.” In Harnack’s telling, this transition from Jewish to Hellenistic influence was initiated by the Apostle Paul. By contrast, current scholarly accounts of Paul’s relationship to Judaism are far more complex: Yes, Paul argued for the inclusion of Gentiles, but not in order to abandon or replace those who saw themselves within Judaism. In other words, Paul believed that those within the non-Christian Jewish community were still the people of God, with whom God was still in covenant. In a theological register one might use today, both the Christian and Jewish communities are waiting for the fulfilment of Israel’s hope. The Church waits through faith in Jesus’s Resurrection; the Synagogues, through study of Torah. Harnack insisted that Paul started to draw the lines between the communities. He conceived the Gospel as the message of redemption already effected by the death and Resurrection of Christ. Seeing the Gospel as a “new force abolishing the religion of the law,” Paul, in Harnack’s reading, understood that religion in its new phase pertained to the individual and therefore to all individuals. 

Contemporary scholars of late-antique Christianity and Judaism are largely skeptical that any single story can be told about the parting of the ways—or even that “Christianity” and “Judaism” were ever monolithic entities. Christianity and Judaism did go separate ways but at different speeds and to varying degrees, depending on location. And in both Judaism and Christianity, Hellenic culture and thought that had been in wide circulation since the age of Alexander the Great, when so many cultures came into contact, had become deeply embedded in Christian discourse by the mid-first century, and even earlier in Jewish discourse. It is now far more common to speak about Judaism and Christianity (and later Islam) in something like the “co-production” model proposed by Katharina Heyden and David Nirenberg. According to this model, Judaism, Christianity, and Islam have always formed, reformed, and transformed themselves in conversation. Far from clarifying things, the “simplicity” of Harnack’s Gospel obscures by removing from view the many ways in which Christianity resists clean separation from Judaism, Hellenism, and the broader Mediterranean culture. 

Although Harnack’s pursuit of a Jesus free of dogma and a Christianity distinct from Judaism would, like the earlier work of the French biblical scholar Ernest Renan in his Life of Jesus, provide ballast to later Nazi theories on the origins of Christianity, that was far from the German scholar’s intent. Indeed, during his lifetime, Harnack was well-known for battling against the forces of antisemitism and was critical in securing university positions for several Jewish colleagues. Less well-known today is Harnack’s role as one of the drafters of the Weimar Constitution, which formally granted Jews full legal rights. This is not to say that Harnack’s lectures received no criticism from Jewish quarters. Perhaps one of the sharpest responses came from Leo Baeck, a greatly respected leader of Reform Judaism in Berlin. Baeck pointed out the many ways in which Harnack failed to take into account the Jewish spiritual life at the time of Jesus, as well as the many places in both the Hebrew Scriptures and the Talmud where the Jewish tradition espouses views that Harnack ascribed exclusively to the essence of Christianity.

Baeck’s sharpest criticism was aimed at Harnack’s claims to historical objectivity, even when he was offering a subjective view—an apologia. Baeck thought it was fine if Harnack wanted to present his view of Christianity, but Christians were not the only ones who could play that game: “Why should [Jews] not be allowed to emphasize the essential, why should they not have the fresh eye for the living?” Baeck asked. “Is that perhaps only a privilege of Protestant theology professors?” On the success of his widely read critique of Harnack’s lectures, Baeck himself would go on to publish The Essence of Judaism, in 1905. Many of the fundamental ideas that Harnack attributed to Christianity—the worth and dignity of every individual human soul, the priority given to disposition in moral action, and the tracing of all morality back to love—Baeck argued were nothing other than Jewish ethics as found in the Talmud. 

But why did Baeck want to establish an essence of Judaism? As the theologian and philosopher of culture Ernst Troeltsch pointed out, the hunt for the “essence” of Christianity was a program of liberal Protestantism, a tradition stretching back at least to Friedrich Schleiermacher. The very phrase “essence of Christianity” was linked, Troeltsch argued, to a modern, critical, and evolutionary history. The historical and philological tools refined throughout the nineteenth century had shown, according to their makers, that Christianity had undergone so much change and transformation that the question of whether the faith today was the same as the one Jesus taught, and the early church believed, had to be answered in the negative. In that respect, Harnack’s quest for the essence was continuous with John Henry Newman’s influential effort to determine what constituted authentic development within Catholic doctrine. Harnack and Newman—and one must also include Harnack’s most persuasive critic, the Catholic modernist Alfred Loisy—were addressing the rise of historical criticism that highlighted many discontinuities and even corruptions within the transmission of the faith through history. To a skeptic, it all seemed a product of political power to say, for example, whose view of Christ’s two natures became “orthodox.” Each of the theologians, in his own way, attempted to answer the question affirmatively. However, to look for the essence of Christianity was a particularly Protestant response to the intellectual challenges of modernity and historicism—and to the skepticism both unleashed. 

Later, in the second half of the twentieth century, it became popular to dismiss “essence” talk. Some of this was motivated by a Wittgensteinian understanding of words as having meaning within the context of use—to speak of essences is to flatten out the differences found localized in particular contexts of use. But the problem that essence-talk was meant to address—how do we conceive of contemporary faith being contiguous with that of the founding epoch of Christianity—never went away; theologians just decided to move on. What recent theology gave up is the ability to provide an account of what a “mere” Christianity might consist in, to borrow from C.S. Lewis. However, such efforts—Harnack’s, Schleiermacher’s, even Lewis’s—never seemed to shake the inessential; they just disguised them in a new language or husk. This is captured by Harnack’s own ambivalence toward the “husk.” It is, in one respect, a blockage to and corruption of the “essence” of the Gospel. But it is also absolutely necessary. Explaining why Christianity shed one husk to acquire another, Harnack wrote that “no religious movement can remain in a bodiless condition.” An essentialized faith is thus doomed to replicate some version of the surrounding culture. It would not be fair to call Harnack an antisemite by the standards of his day, but it is clear that he was unable to break out of a German culture in which the devaluation, isolation, and, ultimately, persecution of Judaism was a matter of course. And this had consequences far beyond the politics of the university and intellectual exchange.

Susannah Heschel, Jewish scholar and daughter of the famous rabbi Abraham Joshua Heschel, has made the astute point that the attempts to eradicate Jewish elements from Christianity amounted to a kind of “theological bulimia.” Nearly every central concept of Christianity relies on some Jewish concept to such an extent that affirming something central to Christian teaching typically entails an affirmation of a Jewish idea or text. This was the point Baeck was attempting to make in his response to Harnack when he cataloged where in the Old Testament many of Jesus’s “essential” sayings could be sourced. But Heschel’s image highlights the inevitable self-harm to which such efforts lead: To eradicate Jewish elements in Christianity is to eviscerate the Christian faith itself. While Harnack was never engaged in any effort to eradicate Jewish elements from Christianity in the same way Nazi scholars did, he did suggest in several places that the Old Testament was an optional part of Scripture and that the “real message” was found in the New Testament. Like Marcion of Sinope, the second-century gnostic, Harnack seemed unaware that downgrading the Old Testament or removing it from the Christian Scriptures would, from a theological standpoint, cut off Christianity at the knees. Indeed, central concepts such as “redemption,” and even the category of “Messiah,” would be rendered incoherent.

The power of Harnack’s argument is that it rendered his thesis on the “simplicity” of the Gospel in strongly historicist terms. He was able to draw not only on his immense knowledge of the history of Christianity but also on a broader cultural discourse that saw in historical reflection a way to check the abuses of tradition and dogma. In the second half of the nineteenth century, this historicist project was shared across the humanities and social sciences. But one of the paradoxes of such efforts is that stripping away all the accretions or supposedly inessential elements from a religion removes those very elements that allow us to track the historical through lines and continuities that link the present worshipping community with those from the earliest periods of the Church. 

It is for this reason that the theological vocabulary as well as the various religious practices and rituals might be more necessary than they at first seem. Harnack took the opportunity in several places in his lectures to point out how the technical theological language, the development of ritual practices, and the various efforts of the Church to manifest itself in institutions and organizations were accretions that distorted the essential message of Jesus. But what this obscures from his view is how that effort subverted the theological discourse within Christianity, to name one such accretion, to question the authority and action of a political regime. The problem with “essence” talk is not that the essence takes a decidedly “liberal” shape but that it has stripped away many of the theological resources that would help identify and resist the self-subversion that Heschel pointed out. Theological vocabulary can provide a language that thwarts the influence of the dominant culture, providing resources for critiquing it. By de-Judaizing Christianity, one is at the same time de-theologizing it, stripping it of the very resources that would protect it against hostile takeovers, including by those who might turn Christianity against the Jewish people. 

Harnack was right to sense that Germany did not need some general theory of society or even a grand new Kantian accounting of the norms that undergird it. In deep crises, like the kind that we are experiencing today, a spiritual response is necessary. However, a spiritual response is only as good as what surrounds it and gives it ballast. Harnack’s critical flaw—the point on which he was most naive—was his conviction that abandoning the traditions that have formed and guided Christians over time would liberate Christianity. Instead, it made it even more vulnerable to exploitation by a political regime. The fate of Harnack’s project is a cautionary tale not just for “liberal” Christianities but any Christianity that might seek to strip away its traditions—intellectual, ritual, and moral—in service of a political imperium.
